attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of retrosternal pain, worse after meals. The pain starts in his upper abdomen and radiates to his chest. He has a history of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and treats himself intermittently with pantoprazole 20 mg daily. He denies weight loss, regurgitation, difficulty swallowing, or breathlessness. His medical history includes a 40 pack-year smoking history with a chronic cough. He consumes three standard drinks of alcohol daily and does not take any other regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His BMI is 32 kg/m2. An electrocardiogram is normal. Endoscopy reveals circumferential ulcerative oesophagitis immediately above a 3 cm hiatus hernia. Biopsies confirm inflammatory changes only.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Prescribe ranitidine in addition to pantoprazole
Arrange oesophageal pH and manometry studies
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with endoscopically-confirmed oesophagitis due to inadequately treated gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD). The presence of a small hiatus hernia and multiple lifestyle risk factors (obesity, smoking, alcohol) are contributing to his symptoms. The first priority in management is to optimise medical therapy while addressing modifiable risk factors.

Increasing pantoprazole to 40 mg daily is the most appropriate next step. The current dose is submaximal, and optimising proton pump inhibitor therapy along with lifestyle modifications (weight loss, smoking cessation, alcohol reduction) should be attempted before considering other interventions. Standard dose proton pump inhibitor therapy heals oesophagitis in approximately 80-85% of patients within eight weeks.

Prescribing ranitidine in addition to a proton pump inhibitor provides no additional benefit. H2-receptor antagonists are less effective than proton pump inhibitors for oesophagitis, healing only 50% of cases. Additionally, tachyphylaxis develops within two weeks of regular H2-receptor antagonist use.

Referral for hiatus hernia repair would be premature. Surgery is reserved for patients who fail maximal medical therapy or develop complications such as stricture or Barrett's oesophagus. Additionally, a 3 cm hiatus hernia is relatively small and does not necessitate surgical intervention. Current guidelines suggest considering surgery only for hernias >5 cm or those with refractory symptoms despite optimal medical management.

Oesophageal pH and manometry studies would only be indicated if considering surgical intervention after failed medical management, which has not yet been optimised in this case. These studies are used to confirm the diagnosis of GORD and evaluate oesophageal motility before anti-reflux surgery, but are not routinely required for typical GORD with endoscopic evidence of oesophagitis.

Adding an over-the-counter antacid provides only temporary symptom relief and does not address the underlying pathology. While antacids can be useful for breakthrough symptoms, they do not promote healing of oesophagitis. The priority is to optimise the more effective proton pump inhibitor therapy.
learningPoints: Lifestyle modifications and optimizing proton pump inhibitor therapy are first-line treatments for GORD with oesophagitis.
Surgery should only be considered after failure of maximal medical therapy in GORD.
Small hiatus hernias (<5 cm) do not require surgical intervention unless symptoms persist despite maximal medical therapy.
references: Fass R. Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease. N Engl J Med 2022;387:1207-16.
Keung C, Hebbard G. The management of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease. Aust Prescr 2016;39:36-9. Available from: https://doi.org/10.18773/austprescr.2016.003
